Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order
Off Calendar Sep-05-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: AMENDED MOTION FOR PROTECTIVE ORDER BY DEFENDANT SERVICELINK AGENCY SALES AND POSTING LLC

No appearances. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART.

At issue is the scope of a PMK deposition of Defendant, a corporate entity. With respect to the deposition of an entity, the deposing party must identify the matters, or topics, covered and the entity shall designate and produce at the deposition those of its officers, directors, managing agents, employees, or agents who are most qualified to testify on its behalf as to those matters to the extent of any information known or reasonably available to the deponent. (See Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.230.) Upon a showing of good cause, the court may issue a protective order protecting a party or deponent from "unwarranted annoyance, embarrassment, or oppression, or undue burden and expense." (Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.420(b).) The available relief includes limiting the scope of the examination and prohibiting inquiry into certain matters. (Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.420(b)(9) and (b)(10).) "The court shall limit the scope of discovery if it determines that the burden, expense, or intrusiveness of that discovery clearly outweighs the likelihood that the information sought will lead to the discovery of admissible evidence." (Code of Civil Procedure section 2017.020(a).) Relief is also available when the discovery sought "is obtainable from some other source that is more convenient, less burdensome, or less expensive." (Code of Civil Procedure section 2019.030(a).) "As between parties to litigation and nonparties, the burden of discovery should be placed on the latter only if the former do not possess the material sought to be discovered." (Calcor Space Facility, Inc. v. Superior Court (1997) 53 Cal.App.4th 216, 225.) In other words, inherent in the Civil Discovery Act is a duty to exhaust party sources before turning to non-parties for information.

Defendant demonstrated adequate meet and confer.

The request for a protective order as to Topics Nos. 2, 3, 4, 11 and 14 is denied. Plaintiffs seek Defendant's knowledge and documents on these topics and these are topics that are likely within Defendant's knowledge and experience. To the extent Defendant is without knowledge or documents as to one or all, it can so testify/state. Defendant contends that these topics require it to produce a witness to testify regarding the knowledge (and produce documents belonging to) a different corporate entity. Reasonably construed Plaintiff's notice/subpoena seeks Defendant's, not some other corporate entity's, knowledge and documents on these topics. Of course, Defendant's information on these topics may be limited, but Plaintiffs rightfully seek the information that a party can provide before turning to third party discovery.

The request for a protective order as to Topic 10 is granted. This topic clearly seeks another entity's knowledge and documents. The Civil Discovery Act does not require a party responding to a PMK notice/subpoena to seek information from other corporate entities or provide information (under oath) not reasonably available to the party. Defendant has established that SeviceLink Title Company is a distinct corporate entity and Defendant has no more access to the information than Plaintiff. Plaintiff did not show that the information sought by way of Topic No. 10 is known or reasonably available to the deponent.

The request for sanctions is denied. Both sides acted with substantial justification. Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order in compliance with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported
